service_modules.name: Intake and demand shaping
service_modules.description: How work enters the system, how demand is interpreted, and how incoming load is shaped before delivery begins.

service_modules.name: Promise and expectation setting
service_modules.description: How the business sets timing, scope, readiness, and client expectations before and during service.

service_modules.name: Capacity and deployment
service_modules.description: How labor, attention, time, and coverage are allocated to fit the actual work pattern.

service_modules.name: Readiness and setup
service_modules.description: How people, stations, materials, and environments are prepared before live execution starts.

service_modules.name: Live execution
service_modules.description: How work is performed in the moment under pressure, variation, interruptions, and real customer impact.

service_modules.name: Handoffs and coordination
service_modules.description: How information, timing, ownership, and responsibility move across roles, nodes, or teams.

service_modules.name: Quality and standards
service_modules.description: How the system maintains standard work, consistency, visible quality, and adherence under load.

service_modules.name: Recovery and exception handling
service_modules.description: How the business detects, absorbs, escalates, and recovers when delivery or expectations break.

service_modules.name: Team capability and training
service_modules.description: How people are prepared, reinforced, coached, and kept execution-ready over time.

service_modules.name: Leadership and management rhythm
service_modules.description: How leaders create direction, presence, review cadence, escalation structure, and follow-through.

service_modules.name: Tools and asset reliability
service_modules.description: How the business ensures that tools, systems, equipment, and supporting assets are available and dependable.

service_modules.name: Commercial and control loops
service_modules.description: How the business monitors losses, stock, waste, margin, leakage, and recurring operational drift.

service_modules.name: Learning and improvement
service_modules.description: How the system reflects on outcomes, updates standards, and compounds operational learning.

service_modules.name: People intelligence
service_modules.description: How team behavior, workload, friction, and engagement are observed, tracked, and acted on to sustain performance and well-being.

failure_families.name: Demand-capacity mismatch
failure_families.description: The incoming load, timing, or complexity exceeds the coverage, skill, or deployment available.

failure_families.name: Expectation-setting failure
failure_families.description: The business promises timing, scope, or experience it cannot reliably deliver in the current operating state.

failure_families.name: Readiness failure
failure_families.description: Teams or environments enter live work without the needed setup, preparation, or preconditions.

failure_families.name: Role clarity failure
failure_families.description: Ownership, responsibilities, escalation paths, or role boundaries are not explicit enough.

failure_families.name: Workflow design failure
failure_families.description: The operating sequence itself is poorly designed, too brittle, or too dependent on heroic effort.

failure_families.name: Handoff failure
failure_families.description: Information, timing, or responsibility transfer breaks between people, stages, or service nodes.

failure_families.name: Standards drift
failure_families.description: Daily execution no longer matches the intended standard, especially under pressure or variation.

failure_families.name: Capability gap
failure_families.description: The team lacks the skill, practice, or reinforcement to execute reliably.

failure_families.name: Leadership rhythm failure
failure_families.description: Managers are not creating enough cadence, presence, review, direction, or escalation structure.

failure_families.name: Control-loop failure
failure_families.description: The business is not measuring, reviewing, or correcting recurring operational loss or drift.

failure_families.name: Tool or resource friction
failure_families.description: Teams are slowed or destabilized by missing, unreliable, ill-fitted, or poorly managed tools and resources.

failure_families.name: Asset reliability failure
failure_families.description: Critical equipment, systems, or operational assets cannot be relied on at the moment they are needed.

failure_families.name: Exception recovery failure
failure_families.description: The organization handles disruptions, complaints, or breakdowns reactively instead of recovering cleanly.

failure_families.name: Commercial discipline failure
failure_families.description: Pricing, mix, waste, stock, time use, or margin discipline is too weak to protect performance.

failure_families.name: Accountability failure
failure_families.description: Work is not owned, checked, followed through, or closed with enough discipline.

failure_families.name: Learning failure
failure_families.description: The same issues repeat because the system does not convert outcomes into updated behavior or standards.

response_logics.name: Stabilize visibility
response_logics.description: Make the live operating state visible fast enough that the team stops flying blind.

response_logics.name: Constrain promises and reset expectations
response_logics.description: Pull promises back inside real delivery capability and make expectations explicit again.

response_logics.name: Align roles and coverage
response_logics.description: Reset ownership, deployment, and coverage so the work has an executable operating shape.

response_logics.name: Restore readiness
response_logics.description: Re-establish the preconditions, preparation, and setup needed before live work continues.

response_logics.name: Redesign workflow
response_logics.description: Change the operating sequence itself so the work becomes more robust, less fragile, and easier to execute.

response_logics.name: Strengthen handoffs
response_logics.description: Improve transfer points so information and responsibility move cleanly between roles and stages.

response_logics.name: Standardize execution
response_logics.description: Reduce unwanted variation by making the standard explicit, visible, and repeatable.

response_logics.name: Install operating rhythm
response_logics.description: Create repeatable cadence, managerial routines, and follow-through structure.

response_logics.name: Tighten control loops
response_logics.description: Measure, review, and correct the recurring leaks or deviations that keep reappearing.

response_logics.name: Build capability
response_logics.description: Raise execution quality through training, coaching, rehearsal, and reinforcement.

response_logics.name: Remove friction and restore reliability
response_logics.description: Remove the equipment, system, or resource frictions that keep degrading execution.

response_logics.name: Reduce operational complexity
response_logics.description: Simplify the offer, flow, or decision surface so the system can perform reliably.

response_logics.name: Strengthen recovery
response_logics.description: Give the team a cleaner way to detect, own, escalate, and recover when things go wrong.

response_logics.name: Verify and learn
response_logics.description: Confirm whether the intervention worked and turn execution outcomes into updated operating truth.

signal_archetypes.name: Demand spike goes unabsorbed
signal_archetypes.description: Incoming work volume surges faster than the operation can absorb it cleanly.
evidence_examples: Orders, arrivals, bookings, or requests bunch into a short window.
Queues expand sharply and the team cannot stabilize flow fast enough.

signal_archetypes.name: Last-minute volume shift hits the operation
signal_archetypes.description: The shape of incoming work changes abruptly and the system cannot adapt in time.
evidence_examples: Late waves, cancellations, walk-ins, or urgent requests distort the expected flow.
The team is surprised by the timing or concentration of work.

signal_archetypes.name: Work intake is not prioritized
signal_archetypes.description: Incoming work is accepted or handled without enough prioritization, triage, or shaping.
evidence_examples: Everything is treated as equally urgent.
There is no clear intake rule for what gets handled first.

signal_archetypes.name: Promised time is missed
signal_archetypes.description: The business commits to timing that actual delivery fails to meet.
evidence_examples: Quoted wait times, delivery times, or completion times slip materially.
Customers are surprised because the stated timeline was not real.

signal_archetypes.name: Scope or offer is misaligned to reality
signal_archetypes.description: The operation is selling or agreeing to something the current system cannot deliver reliably.
evidence_examples: The offer is too broad, too complex, or too custom for the current operating strength.
Teams repeatedly say yes to work that the backend cannot support.

signal_archetypes.name: Customer expectation surprise appears
signal_archetypes.description: Customers react as if the delivered experience differed meaningfully from what they thought they were buying.
evidence_examples: Complaints indicate surprise about timing, process, price, or service shape.
Frontline teams spend time re-explaining what should have been clear earlier.

signal_archetypes.name: Peak period is undercovered
signal_archetypes.description: The system enters a busy period with too little coverage for the work pattern.
evidence_examples: Critical moments consistently feel short-staffed or unsupported.
Backlogs form because there is not enough coverage where the work is happening.

signal_archetypes.name: Skill mix is mismatched to the work
signal_archetypes.description: The people available do not match the type or complexity of work landing in that period.
evidence_examples: Too many inexperienced people are handling the hardest moments.
The right people are present but not deployed into the right roles.

signal_archetypes.name: Critical role is left uncovered
signal_archetypes.description: A role essential to stable delivery is absent, split, or left ambiguous.
evidence_examples: No one clearly owns a key node or handoff point.
A high-leverage role is pulled away without replacement.

signal_archetypes.name: Shift or day starts unprepared
signal_archetypes.description: Live work begins before the team or environment is actually ready.
evidence_examples: The day opens without a completed setup, briefing, or readiness check.
Teams start serving or delivering while still assembling basics.

signal_archetypes.name: Required inputs are missing at start
signal_archetypes.description: Essential information, materials, tools, or prep inputs are absent when work begins.
evidence_examples: Teams have to stop and search for things they should already have.
The work begins before prerequisites are in place.

signal_archetypes.name: Opening or reset is incomplete
signal_archetypes.description: The environment is not fully reset between cycles, shifts, or days.
evidence_examples: The next team inherits unresolved prep, mess, stock gaps, or unclear status.
Opening or closing routines are inconsistently completed.

signal_archetypes.name: Service or job cycle delay appears
signal_archetypes.description: The core delivery cycle slows beyond what the customer or downstream system can absorb.
evidence_examples: Tickets, appointments, jobs, or task cycles take materially longer than expected.
Delivery speed visibly drops under real demand.

signal_archetypes.name: Rework or repeat work is observed
signal_archetypes.description: The operation is doing the same work twice because the first pass did not hold.
evidence_examples: Corrections, remakes, re-cleans, or duplicate touches happen in normal flow.
Teams re-enter the same task because it was not truly finished the first time.

signal_archetypes.name: Work pace breaks under load
signal_archetypes.description: Execution becomes unstable or fragmented when demand or complexity rises.
evidence_examples: Teams can cope when quiet but lose coherence when busy.
Throughput collapses disproportionately during the peak period.

signal_archetypes.name: Handoff requires re-clarification
signal_archetypes.description: Transferred work repeatedly needs clarification because the first handoff was incomplete or unclear.
evidence_examples: People have to ask again what is needed, who owns it, or what changed.
Work slows because the next role does not receive a usable handoff.

signal_archetypes.name: Ownership confusion appears during flow
signal_archetypes.description: People are unsure who owns the next action or who should intervene at a critical moment.
evidence_examples: Multiple people assume someone else owns the next step.
A task sits because responsibility is ambiguous.

signal_archetypes.name: Work dies between roles
signal_archetypes.description: A piece of work loses momentum or disappears when it passes from one role to another.
evidence_examples: Tasks stall at boundaries between teams or stages.
Important details are lost when ownership changes hands.

signal_archetypes.name: Output quality is inconsistent
signal_archetypes.description: Customers or managers experience uneven output quality for similar work.
evidence_examples: Results differ too much by person, shift, site, or moment.
Good outcomes are possible but not reliably repeated.

signal_archetypes.name: Standard steps are skipped
signal_archetypes.description: Teams omit required standard steps in normal operation, especially under pressure.
evidence_examples: Critical checks or sequence steps are bypassed.
The expected standard exists on paper but not in live flow.

signal_archetypes.name: Checker or quality control is absent
signal_archetypes.description: No reliable mechanism exists to catch quality drift before it reaches the customer or downstream team.
evidence_examples: Work leaves a stage without verification.
Defects are discovered only after the fact.

signal_archetypes.name: Issue resolution is slow or unclear
signal_archetypes.description: When something goes wrong, the path to resolution is too slow, too vague, or too improvisational.
evidence_examples: Teams hesitate or escalate too late when problems happen.
Recovery depends on who happens to be present.

signal_archetypes.name: Complaint is handled defensively
signal_archetypes.description: Customer-facing recovery creates friction because the response is defensive, fragmented, or impersonal.
evidence_examples: The team explains instead of recovering.
Customers feel resisted before they feel helped.

signal_archetypes.name: Exception repeats without containment
signal_archetypes.description: A known exception keeps recurring because the system never contains the underlying pattern.
evidence_examples: The same complaint or disruption happens again within a short period.
Recovery resolves the moment but not the repeatability.

signal_archetypes.name: New staff depend on memory and verbal instruction
signal_archetypes.description: New or less experienced team members rely on memory, guessing, or ad hoc explanation instead of structured support.
evidence_examples: People keep asking basic operational questions live.
Onboarding depends on whoever is nearby rather than an explicit method.

signal_archetypes.name: Same mistake repeats across people
signal_archetypes.description: Different people reproduce the same error pattern, suggesting the issue is systemic rather than individual.
evidence_examples: Multiple staff make the same mistake across shifts.
The problem survives staff changes.

signal_archetypes.name: Training is not translating into execution
signal_archetypes.description: People have been told or shown what to do, but live behavior still does not reflect the standard.
evidence_examples: The team can explain the standard but does not hold it under pressure.
Training happened once, but reinforcement is missing.

signal_archetypes.name: Manager is absent from a critical moment
signal_archetypes.description: The operation reaches a high-risk moment without visible managerial presence or direction.
evidence_examples: Pressure rises and no leader is actively steering the moment.
Escalations happen after the damage instead of during the buildup.

signal_archetypes.name: No live direction or reset occurs
signal_archetypes.description: The team stays in drift because no one stops, resets, or reorients execution in the moment.
evidence_examples: Problems continue accumulating without a clear operational reset.
The team feels left to self-manage through a deteriorating situation.

signal_archetypes.name: Follow-through is missing
signal_archetypes.description: Managers or leaders name issues but do not reliably close them.
evidence_examples: Problems are discussed repeatedly but still open.
Agreements exist without disciplined follow-up.

signal_archetypes.name: Tool or system is unavailable when needed
signal_archetypes.description: A critical system, tool, or operational support element is missing at the moment of use.
evidence_examples: Teams stop to find access, logins, materials, or forms.
Execution waits because a supposedly basic tool is not ready.

signal_archetypes.name: Equipment or asset breaks the flow
signal_archetypes.description: An unreliable physical or digital asset interrupts the operating sequence.
evidence_examples: Equipment failure or system instability creates live delay.
A key asset becomes a bottleneck or failure point.

signal_archetypes.name: Workaround has become normal
signal_archetypes.description: Teams routinely compensate for broken tools, missing supports, or unreliable assets through informal workarounds.
evidence_examples: People describe hacks as standard practice.
The operation functions by compensating around a known weakness.

signal_archetypes.name: Stock or input accuracy is weak
signal_archetypes.description: The business does not have a reliable view of what inputs, stock, or resources are actually available.
evidence_examples: Teams discover shortages or mismatches live.
Counts, availability, or records repeatedly differ from reality.

signal_archetypes.name: Waste, loss, or leakage is unexplained
signal_archetypes.description: Operational loss is visible, but the business cannot explain, localize, or correct it cleanly.
evidence_examples: Margin leakage, waste, shrinkage, or rework is known but not controlled.
Losses are discussed as normal rather than investigated.

signal_archetypes.name: Key operating metric is not reviewed
signal_archetypes.description: A critical operating measure exists but is not reviewed with enough discipline to drive correction.
evidence_examples: Teams collect numbers without acting on them.
Leaders do not regularly review the metrics that would reveal the problem.

signal_archetypes.name: Problem is discussed but not closed
signal_archetypes.description: The organization talks about a recurring issue without resolving it into an updated operating truth.
evidence_examples: The same issue appears in repeated conversations.
Awareness exists, but closure and system change do not.

signal_archetypes.name: No postmortem follows a breakdown
signal_archetypes.description: A meaningful failure happens and the business does not review it deeply enough to learn.
evidence_examples: Breakdowns are survived and forgotten.
No structured reflection happens after a serious service miss.

signal_archetypes.name: Improvement is not standardized
signal_archetypes.description: Something improved locally, but the gain was never turned into repeatable operating practice.
evidence_examples: One person or shift fixed it, but the fix did not travel.
Better behavior remains informal instead of becoming standard.

signal_failure_hypotheses.rationale: A spike the system cannot absorb is the clearest sign that incoming load exceeds available coverage or sequence capacity.
If spikes collapse the flow immediately, the operating sequence itself is likely too brittle or poorly buffered.
Late demand shape changes often expose a system that cannot reallocate capacity fast enough.
Sudden shifts become more damaging when the business keeps promising as if the original shape still applies.
Weak triage means the intake flow itself is not designed to shape work into an executable sequence.
Prioritization often breaks when leaders are not creating enough live review and decision cadence.
Missing promised timing means the promise was not set inside real delivery capability.
Timing promises are especially vulnerable when deployed capacity cannot absorb the actual load.
Offer misalignment directly reflects a promise surface that is detached from operational reality.
Over-broad or overly custom offers often reflect weak discipline around what the business should really sell.
Customer surprise is the cleanest sign that expectations were not formed or reset correctly.
Expectation gaps also appear when important context is lost between booking, front line, and delivery.
Visible undercoverage at the peak is a direct expression of supply-demand mismatch.
Weak deployment review and forecasting cadence often leaves the peak moment exposed.
If the available team lacks the right skill mix, the system is carrying a real execution capability gap.
A coverage plan can fail even with enough bodies if the deployed skill profile does not fit the work.
Uncovered critical roles usually mean ownership boundaries were not designed or protected clearly enough.
Critical roles also go uncovered when overall deployment is too thin for the work shape.
Starting work unprepared is the most direct form of readiness failure.
Readiness gaps persist when leaders do not enforce briefings, resets, and opening discipline.
Missing prerequisites at the start show the system entered live work before setup was complete.
If inputs are habitually unavailable, the operation is also carrying friction in its support resources.
Incomplete reset breaks the transition into the next cycle before work even begins.
If resets keep failing, the business likely lacks a review loop that checks and corrects that routine.
Cycle delay is often what customers see first when demand outstrips delivery capacity.
Persistent delay also suggests the sequence or handoff shape of the work is structurally weak.
Repeat work is a strong sign that the intended standard is not holding on first pass.
Rework can also mean the workflow itself is forcing duplicate touches or avoidable loops.
If pace collapses under load, the system cannot carry that level of demand with current deployment.
Load-sensitive breakdowns also indicate fragile sequencing and weak operational buffering.
Repeated clarification is a direct sign that the first transfer was incomplete or unusable.
Handoffs become muddy when role boundaries and responsibility lines are not explicit.
Live confusion about who owns the next action points straight at weak role clarity.
Ownership confusion often appears at the exact moment responsibility should transfer cleanly.
When work dies at boundaries, the transfer mechanism itself is failing.
Dropped work also reflects weak follow-through once ownership is supposed to pass.
Inconsistent output strongly suggests the operating standard is not holding across people and moments.
Variation also grows when the team cannot execute the standard with enough skill.
Skipping required steps is one of the clearest forms of drift from standard work.
Steps continue getting skipped when no one is truly checking and holding the line.
Without a check step, quality drift reaches the customer or downstream team too easily.
Missing verification also reflects a weak control loop around quality and defects.
Slow or vague recovery shows the business does not have a clean exception-handling method.
Recovery weakens further when leaders are not visibly steering or escalating the issue.
Defensive complaint handling is a frontline sign of poor recovery design.
It also often indicates the team lacks coached recovery behavior under pressure.
Recurring unresolved exceptions show the recovery pattern is not containing the issue.
If the same disruption repeats, the system is not learning from previous breakdowns.
Memory-based onboarding is a strong sign that the team lacks structured execution capability.
When standards are not externalized, they drift person by person.
The same repeated mistake across multiple people suggests a systemic skill and reinforcement gap.
If the pattern repeats broadly, the system is also failing to convert mistakes into corrected practice.
Training that does not survive into live execution is still a capability problem.
Translation from training to execution usually needs managerial reinforcement and review cadence.
Absent leadership at the critical moment directly reflects a broken management rhythm.
Peak absence becomes more damaging when the underlying load already exceeds system stability.
If nobody resets the moment, the system lacks real-time leadership cadence.
Drift persists because no one is clearly holding the next correction step.
Poor follow-through is a classic sign that review and closure rhythms are weak.
Open loops persist because the system is not truly owning completion.
If a needed tool is not there at the moment of use, operational friction is already harming execution.
Unavailability at start or handoff also reflects incomplete preparation before live work.
An asset that breaks flow is directly failing its reliability role in the system.
Unreliable assets also create daily execution friction even before outright failure.
Normalizing workarounds means teams are repeatedly compensating around structural friction.
Workarounds often grow around assets or systems that cannot be trusted to hold.
Weak stock accuracy reflects a broken loop for measuring, reviewing, and correcting reality.
Stock inaccuracies also signal weak operational discipline around loss, waste, and availability.
Visible unexplained leakage is a direct sign that commercial discipline is not protecting the business.
If leakage is seen but not localized, the control loop is also too weak.
Not reviewing a key metric means the business cannot correct itself from the signals it already has.
Metrics are usually ignored when leaders do not create a review rhythm that forces attention.
Repeated discussion without closure means the system is not converting awareness into learning.
Open loops also reflect weak ownership and completion discipline.
Skipping review after a breakdown is a direct failure to learn from execution reality.
Postmortems usually do not happen when leadership has not built a review ritual.
If a gain never becomes standard, the organization is failing to store and spread learning.
Improvements that stay informal leave the standard vulnerable to drift back.

failure_response_hypotheses.rationale: Load mismatch is first corrected by reshaping coverage and deployment around actual demand.
The team needs live visibility before it can steer a load problem intelligently.
When demand cannot be absorbed, narrowing the decision or offer surface can restore stability.
Expectation failures must first be corrected by bringing promises back inside real delivery capability.
Clear visibility into actual timing and flow helps reset promises honestly.
Readiness failures are best corrected by rebuilding preconditions before live work continues.
Readiness improves when resets, openings, and briefings become disciplined routines.
Role confusion is primarily corrected by making ownership and boundaries explicit in the operating shape.
Role clarity often has to be reinforced at the boundary where work passes to the next owner.
If the sequence itself is weak, the workflow must be redesigned rather than merely pushed harder.
Over-complex flows often need simplification before redesign can hold.
Broken transfers are directly corrected by hardening the handoff itself.
Teams often need shared visibility so the next owner can see what is actually being passed.
Drift is best corrected by making the expected standard visible, teachable, and repeatable again.
Standards need verification loops or they decay back into variation.
Skill and execution gaps require coaching, training, reinforcement, and rehearsal.
People learn faster when the standard is explicit and externally supported.
Weak leadership cadence is directly addressed by creating repeatable review and reset rhythm.
Leaders need a visible operating state before they can direct the moment well.
Control-loop failure should be corrected by restoring measurement, review, and corrective action.
Control loops improve further when the team verifies whether corrections actually changed the outcome.
Execution friction caused by missing supports should be corrected by restoring dependable tools and inputs.
Some friction is really a readiness issue because basic supports were not in place before work began.
Broken or unreliable assets require direct reliability restoration, not just team effort.
In the short term, the operation may need to simplify around an unreliable asset.
Recovery failures should first be corrected by designing clearer ownership and response patterns for exceptions.
Recovery gets stronger when escalation and review rhythm are visible and repeatable.
Commercial leakage usually needs disciplined measurement and review to become controllable.
Complex offers and complex execution often create the commercial leaks in the first place.
Accountability strengthens when follow-through and review become structured rhythms rather than intentions.
People own work better when the operating shape makes ownership unambiguous.
Learning failure is directly addressed by verifying outcomes and storing the lesson in the operating system.
Learning only compounds when improvements are translated into repeatable standard work.

people_signals.name: Repeated task delays
people_signals.description: An individual consistently finishes tasks later than peers on comparable work.

people_signals.name: Overdue follow-up accumulation
people_signals.description: An individual has a rising count of overdue follow-ups over consecutive shifts.

people_signals.name: Escalation frequency spike
people_signals.description: An individual creates or triggers escalations at a rate above the team average.

people_signals.name: Shift coverage gap
people_signals.description: Shifts are under-staffed relative to demand signals in the venue assessment.

people_signals.name: Task refusal pattern
people_signals.description: An individual leaves tasks in not_started or defers them disproportionately.

people_signals.name: High friction report rate
people_signals.description: Anonymous friction reports from a venue exceed a rolling weekly threshold.

people_signals.name: Diary silence
people_signals.description: An individual who previously logged diary entries has stopped for multiple shifts.

people_signals.name: Sub-action completion stall
people_signals.description: An individual starts tasks but never completes more than 50% of sub-actions.

people_signals.name: Cross-venue overload
people_signals.description: A team member assigned across multiple venues shows declining performance in all.

people_signals.name: Manager absent from execution
people_signals.description: The venue manager has not interacted with the execution layer for multiple days.

people_signals.name: Team velocity declining
people_signals.description: The venue team overall completion rate has dropped for two or more consecutive assessment cycles.

people_signals.name: Single point of failure
people_signals.description: One individual handles disproportionate share of critical tasks with no backup.

people_signals.name: Escalation resolution delay
people_signals.description: Owner-level escalations remain open beyond 48 hours without acknowledgment.

people_signals.name: Evidence gap
people_signals.description: Completed tasks lack evidence attachments, suggesting work is reported but not verified.

people_signals.name: Repeated blocked pattern
people_signals.description: Same tasks or task types get blocked across multiple assessment cycles.

people_signals.name: Onboarding friction
people_signals.description: New team members show significantly lower task completion rates in first two weeks.

people_signals.name: Shift handover gap
people_signals.description: Tasks started in one shift are not continued or acknowledged in the next.

people_signals.name: Workload imbalance
people_signals.description: Task assignment is unevenly distributed across team members in the same venue.

people_signals.name: Positive momentum
people_signals.description: An individual or team shows consistent task completion improvement over multiple cycles.

people_signals.name: Help-seeking increase
people_signals.description: An individual accesses the help/copilot feature significantly more than usual.

people_signals.name: Quality without speed
people_signals.description: An individual completes tasks slowly but with full evidence and sub-action completion.

people_signals.name: Speed without quality
people_signals.description: An individual marks tasks complete quickly but skips sub-actions and evidence.

people_signals.name: Flight risk indicator
people_signals.description: Combination of declining engagement, diary silence, and rising friction suggests potential departure.

people_signals.name: Burnout pattern
people_signals.description: High task volume combined with overdue accumulation and declining completion quality.

people_signals.name: Delegation bottleneck
people_signals.description: Owner or manager holds tasks that should be delegated, creating downstream delays.

people_signals.name: Standards compliance gap
people_signals.description: Team members access standards rarely or never, correlated with lower quality outcomes.

people_signals.name: Friction cluster
people_signals.description: Multiple friction reports from different team members point to the same underlying issue.

people_signals.name: Recovery after intervention
people_signals.description: Team or individual performance improves measurably after an escalation is resolved.

people_signals.name: Communication gap
people_signals.description: Low copilot thread activity in a venue despite active execution, suggesting team is working in isolation.

people_signals.name: Seasonal load mismatch
people_signals.description: Historical patterns show recurring capacity problems during predictable high-demand periods.
